chunks.title: Payments Service Authentication
chunks.text: To configure authentication for the payments service, register the service as an OAuth2 client with the identity provider and request a client credential from the platform team. Set AUTH_CLIENT_ID and AUTH_CLIENT_SECRET as environment variables; at startup the service exchanges them for a short-lived bearer token via the client-credentials grant, requesting only the scope its own endpoints need. The token is cached in memory and refreshed a few minutes before it expires, so a slow refresh never opens an authentication gap on the next call. Tokens are never logged and never written to disk; if a refresh fails, the client retries with backoff rather than blocking the request path.

chunks.title: Notifications Retry Policy
chunks.text: The notifications pipeline retries failed deliveries with exponential backoff and full jitter, a pattern chosen specifically to avoid retry storms when many clients back off in lockstep. The base delay is 500 milliseconds; each attempt's wait is chosen uniformly at random up to the doubling delay, for up to five attempts. Randomizing the wait, rather than a fixed doubling delay, spreads retries out in time so a recovering downstream dependency isn't immediately hit by a synchronized wave of retries. After the retry budget is exhausted, the message moves to a dead-letter queue rather than being retried indefinitely. The dead-letter queue is drained by an on-call engineer during business hours, who decides whether to replay, discard, or escalate each message.

chunks.title: Database Failover Procedure
chunks.text: For a database failover, first confirm the primary is unhealthy via the health dashboard and quorum checks, not a single missed heartbeat, to avoid a split-brain promotion. Then promote the standby replica using the failover script and update the connection pool to point at the new primary; expect up to thirty seconds of write unavailability during promotion, the recovery-time objective for this database tier. After failover, open an incident and verify replication has re-established on the demoted node - treat it as a new replica rather than assuming it will silently resume its old role. A brief pause during promotion is the deliberate trade-off: a failover that guesses wrong about which node is primary risks losing acknowledged writes, which is worse than a short, predictable delay.

chunks.title: Gateway Rate Limiting
chunks.text: The gateway rate limiter uses a token bucket per client: a bucket has a fixed capacity and refills at a configured rate, which lets it absorb short bursts while still enforcing a steady average - the property that distinguishes token-bucket limiting from a strict fixed window. Each request consumes one token; when the bucket is empty, the gateway returns HTTP 429 Too Many Requests with a Retry-After header telling the client how long to wait. Buckets are stored in Redis so the limit is enforced globally, shared across every gateway replica, rather than each replica keeping its own local count. Limits are configured per API key in the gateway control plane.

chunks.title: Transfer API Idempotency
chunks.text: The transfer API enforces idempotency through an Idempotency-Key header, the same pattern used by major payment APIs: the client generates one unique key per logical transfer and sends it on every attempt of that same operation. The first request with a given key is processed and its response cached; any later request with the same key returns the cached response without reprocessing, so a client retrying after a timeout can never accidentally move money twice. Keys expire after twenty-four hours. Clients must generate a new key per logical transfer, not per retry attempt - reusing a key across two different transfers, or minting a fresh key on every retry of the same transfer, both defeat the guarantee this header exists to provide.

chunks.title: Credential Rotation
chunks.text: To rotate a service credential, generate a new secret in the vault and deploy it as a new environment variable alongside the old one, so both are simultaneously valid. Confirm the service accepts requests authenticated with either secret, then remove the old one only after confirming - for example from auth logs - that no in-flight traffic is still using it. Never rotate by deleting the old secret first: any request already in flight, or any instance that hasn't yet picked up the new value, fails authentication the moment the old secret disappears. This dual-secret overlap window is what makes rotation zero-downtime. Schedule rotations during low-traffic windows so a surfaced problem has a small blast radius.

chunks.title: Fraud Scoring SLA
chunks.text: The fraud scoring service publishes a p99 latency SLI of 120 milliseconds and an availability objective of 99.95 percent, backed by an error budget: sustained breaches gate further risky releases until the budget recovers. Scoring requests that would exceed the latency budget don't block the transaction; they fall back to a conservative rule-based score instead - the same graceful-degradation trade-off used whenever a fast, approximate path is safer than a slow, exact one under load. The fallback path is monitored separately, since a creeping fallback rate is an early warning the primary scoring path is degrading even while the SLA is still technically met. It alarms separately if it exceeds five percent of traffic, well before that would threaten the SLA itself.

chunks.title: Payments Outage Post-Mortem
chunks.text: This is a blameless post-mortem for the payments outage: it documents contributing factors and process gaps, not individual fault. Summary: a credential rotation on the payments service caused an authentication outage. Root cause: the rotation procedure deleted the old secret before the new one had finished propagating to every instance, so in-flight requests signed with the old secret began failing authentication the moment it was deleted. Detection: an authentication-error-rate alert caught the payments outage within minutes, but a portion of payment traffic had already failed closed by then. Remediation: the rotation procedure now requires a dual-secret window - both secrets valid at once - before the old one is ever removed, closing exactly the gap that caused this outage. This post-mortem, including the root cause and rotation timeline, is restricted to the security team.
